name: Cardinalidae
description: Cardinalidae is a family of New World passerine birds comprising cardinals, grosbeaks, buntings, and the tanagers of the genera Piranga, Habia, and Chlorothraupis, which were moved here from Thraupidae following molecular phylogenetic studies.

children.name: Cardinalis
children.description: The true cardinals — crested, thick-billed passerines named for the brilliant red plumage of the male, which resembles the robes of Catholic cardinals. They are among the most recognisable and popular birds in the Americas, with a rich vocabulary of whistled songs.

children.name: Cardinalis cardinalis
children.description: One of the most beloved and recognisable birds in North America, the male is a brilliant crimson red with black face mask and prominent crest. It has expanded dramatically northward into Canada, aided by backyard bird feeders.

children.name: Cardinalis sinuatus
children.description: The desert cardinal of the southwestern United States and Mexico, distinguished by its greyish body, red face mask and crest, and a thick curved yellow bill that resembles a parrot's.

children.name: Cardinalis phoeniceus
children.description: A striking cardinal of coastal Colombia and Venezuela, with the male entirely vivid vermilion red. It inhabits arid scrub and thorn forest and is classified as Least Concern.

children.name: Pseudocyanocompsa viridis
children.description: The Green-crowned Quail is a medium-sized bird with a distinctive green crown and white throat patch. Found in shrublands and woodland edges, it feeds on seeds and fruits. Males have a bright red breast.

children.name: Granatellus caerulescens
children.description: The Blue-throated Granate is a small bird with a bright blue throat patch and reddish-brown plumage. It inhabits open savannas and grasslands, where it feeds on insects and seeds. Males sing complex songs during breeding.

children.name: Caryothraustes aurantius
children.description: The Golden-crowned Tawnythroat is a medium-sized bird with a distinctive golden crown and brownish-gray plumage. It inhabits woodland edges and shrublands, where it feeds on seeds and fruits. Males have a bright yellow breast.

children.name: Pheucticus
children.description: New World grosbeaks with massive conical bills adapted for cracking hard seeds and nuts. Males are boldly patterned in black, white, and bright yellow or rose-red.

children.name: Pheucticus ludovicianus
children.description: A striking songbird of eastern North American forests, the male is black and white with a vivid triangular rose-red patch on the breast. It winters from Mexico through Central America to the northern Andes.

children.name: Pheucticus melanocephalus
children.description: The western counterpart of the Rose-breasted Grosbeak, with a black head, orange breast, and black-and-white wings. It is one of the few birds that can safely eat monarch butterflies.

children.name: Pheucticus chrysogaster
children.description: A striking yellow-and-black grosbeak of the Andes from Colombia to northern Chile and Argentina, found in montane scrub and forest edges. Its golden-yellow underparts contrast sharply with its black hood and wings.

children.name: Pheucticus aureoventris
children.description: A large grosbeak of Andean cloud forests from Venezuela to Bolivia. The male has a black back, bright yellow underparts, and a thick pale bill; its song is a rich, robin-like warble.

children.name: Pheucticus chrysopeplus
children.description: A large, vividly yellow grosbeak of Pacific slope forests from Mexico to Panama. The male has a yellow body, black wings with white patches, and a massive conical bill. It is local and uncommon in much of its range.

children.name: Pheucticus tibialis
children.description: A little-known grosbeak endemic to the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama. It is similar to the Yellow Grosbeak but has black thighs and a more restricted range in montane cloud forest.

children.name: Passerina
children.description: New World buntings — small, brilliantly coloured seed-eating birds with vivid blues, indigos, and greens in the males. Young males take two years to attain full adult plumage.

children.name: Passerina caerulea
children.description: A large, heavy-billed bunting with deep rich blue plumage and two rusty wing bars. It favours shrubby fields and overgrown pastures across the southern and central United States.

children.name: Passerina cyanea
children.description: A small, vivid blue finch-like bird that appears to glow electric blue in direct sunlight. It navigates at night using the stars during its nocturnal migrations.

children.name: Passerina amoena
children.description: The western counterpart of the Indigo Bunting, with a turquoise-blue back, rusty breast, and white wing bars. It frequently hybridises with the Indigo Bunting in the Great Plains.

children.name: Passerina ciris
children.description: Arguably the most brilliantly coloured bird in North America, combining a blue head, green back, and red underparts. The female is uniform bright green, providing camouflage while incubating.

children.name: Passerina rositae
children.description: A little-known bunting endemic to the Pacific slope of southern Mexico and Guatemala. The male has a blue head, green back, and a rose-pink belly patch. It inhabits arid tropical scrub and is classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Passerina leclancherii
children.description: A stunning bunting endemic to western Mexico, with the male sporting a turquoise-blue head, green back, and vivid orange breast and belly. It inhabits dry deciduous forest and thorn scrub.

children.name: Passerina versicolor
children.description: A bunting of arid scrublands from the southwestern United States through Mexico to Guatemala. The male's plumage combines purple, red, and blue in a variable pattern unique among North American birds.

children.name: Spiza
children.description: A monotypic genus of grassland bird named for its distinctive song — a dry, buzzing 'dick-dick-cissel'. It is locally common but notably irruptive.

children.name: Spiza americana
children.description: A sparrow-sized bird of tallgrass prairies with a yellow breast and black V. It breeds in the central US and winters in vast flocks on the llanos of Venezuela and Colombia.

children.name: Cyanocompsa
children.description: A genus of predominantly blue seedeaters and grosbeaks found in the Neotropics. Males are deep ultramarine blue; females are warm brown.

children.name: Cyanocompsa parellina
children.description: A small, deep-blue bunting of lowland forests and thickets from Mexico to Panama. The male is almost entirely dark blue with a paler blue forehead and shoulders.

children.name: Cyanocompsa brissonii
children.description: A large, thick-billed seedeater of open woodland and savanna in eastern and central South America. The male is brilliant ultramarine blue with black wings and tail.

children.name: Caryothraustes
children.description: A small genus of robust, thick-billed grosbeaks found in lowland forests of Central and South America. They are highly social, often foraging in noisy flocks in the canopy.

children.name: Caryothraustes canadensis
children.description: A striking green-and-yellow grosbeak of lowland rainforest canopy from Panama to the Amazon and Atlantic Forest of Brazil. Its massive pale bill gives it a distinctive profile.

children.name: Caryothraustes poliogaster
children.description: A handsome grosbeak of Caribbean slope lowland forests from southern Mexico to Panama, with a black face mask, yellow-green body, and heavy silver-grey bill.

children.name: Periporphyrus
children.description: A monotypic or small genus of Neotropical grosbeaks with a massive, brightly coloured bill. Found in lowland forests of the Guianas and Amazon.

children.name: Periporphyrus erythromelas
children.description: A stunning lowland forest grosbeak from the Guianas and eastern Amazon, with the male jet black above and rich red below, and a massive ivory-coloured bill.

children.name: Amaurospiza
children.description: A genus of secretive, predominantly blue seedeaters found in dense undergrowth in Central and South American forests. They are difficult to observe and remain poorly known.

children.name: Amaurospiza concolor
children.description: A small, uniformly slate-blue seedeater of montane cloud forest undergrowth from Mexico to Panama. Skulking and difficult to see, it is more often heard than observed.

children.name: Amaurospiza carrizalensis
children.description: A recently described seedeater endemic to bamboo-dominated forests on the slopes of the Cerro Carrizal in Venezuela. It is known from a single location and is considered Critically Endangered.

children.name: Amaurospiza moesta
children.description: A dark, sooty-blue seedeater of Atlantic Forest bamboo thickets in south-eastern Brazil, eastern Paraguay, and northern Argentina. Its population is declining due to habitat loss and it is listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Chlorothraupis
children.description: Medium-sized, olive-and-yellow tanagers formerly placed in Thraupidae but now assigned to Cardinalidae on molecular evidence. They inhabit lowland and foothill forest understorey in Central and South America.

children.name: Chlorothraupis carmioli
children.description: An olive-green tanager with a pale yellowish eye and throat, found in humid lowland forests from Nicaragua through Central America to the Pacific slope of Colombia and Ecuador.

children.name: Chlorothraupis olivacea
children.description: A uniformly olive-green tanager with a distinctive yellow eye-ring, found in lowland forests of the Chocó region in western Colombia and north-western Ecuador.

children.name: Chlorothraupis stolzmanni
children.description: A tanager of the Chocó lowlands, with a warm ochre-yellow breast and olive upperparts. It forages in the understorey of wet forest, often following army ant swarms.

children.name: Chlorothraupis monticola
children.description: The Mountain Emerald is a small bird with bright green plumage and white throat patch. It inhabits mountain forests and shrublands, where it feeds on insects and seeds. Males sing complex songs during breeding.

children.name: Piranga
children.description: A genus of brightly coloured tanagers formerly placed in Thraupidae but now assigned to Cardinalidae on molecular evidence. Males are typically vivid red, orange, or yellow; females are duller.

children.name: Piranga flava
children.description: A stocky, red-orange tanager of open pine-oak woodland and forest edges from the southwestern US through Central America to Argentina. The male has a greyish ear patch and a relatively stout bill.

children.name: Piranga olivacea
children.description: A spectacular migrant tanager, the male is brilliant scarlet with black wings and tail. It breeds in eastern North American deciduous forests and winters in the Andean foothills of South America.

children.name: Piranga ludoviciana
children.description: A striking tanager of western North American coniferous forests, the male has a yellow body, red-orange head, and black wings with bold white wing bars.

children.name: Piranga bidentata
children.description: A fiery orange-red tanager of montane forests from Mexico to Panama, with bold black wing markings. It closely resembles the Western Tanager but lacks white wing bars.

children.name: Piranga leucoptera
children.description: A small, bright red tanager with bold white wing bars, found in montane forests from southern Mexico to Bolivia. It often joins mixed-species flocks in the canopy.

children.name: Piranga erythrocephala
children.description: A Mexican endemic with a bright red head and throat, yellow underparts, and olive back. It is found in pine-oak and cloud forests of the Sierra Madre Occidental and Oriental.

children.name: Piranga rubriceps
children.description: A stunning, entirely crimson-red tanager with black lores and a pinkish bill, found in montane cloud forests of the Colombian and Ecuadorian Andes.

children.name: Piranga rubra
children.description: The only entirely red bird in North America (male). It breeds in open woodlands across the southern and eastern United States and winters from Mexico to central South America.

children.name: Piranga roseogularis
children.description: A little-known tanager of the Yucatán Peninsula and northern Guatemala. The male has a rose-pink throat and breast, grey upperparts, and a white belly.

children.name: Habia
children.description: Medium-sized, dark-plumaged tanagers formerly placed in Thraupidae but now assigned to Cardinalidae. They are associated with dense undergrowth and often follow army ant swarms.

children.name: Habia rubica
children.description: A dark red-brown tanager with a concealed red crown patch, found in lowland forest understorey from Mexico to Argentina. It is a regular attendant at army ant swarms.

children.name: Habia fuscicauda
children.description: A dark olive-brown tanager with a vivid red throat and breast, found in humid lowland forests from southern Mexico to Panama. Its loud, chattering calls are distinctive.

children.name: Habia atrimaxillaris
children.description: A rare tanager endemic to the Osa Peninsula of Costa Rica, with a black face mask, dark red throat, and olive-brown body. It is listed as Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Habia gutturalis
children.description: A dark, sooty-grey tanager with a rose-red throat patch, found in the wet lowland forests of the Chocó region in Colombia and north-western Ecuador.

children.name: Habia cristata
children.description: A striking black-and-red tanager endemic to the Colombian Andes, with a prominent crest and bright red underparts. It inhabits the understorey of humid montane forests.

children.name: Granatellus
children.description: A genus of small, colourful chats formerly placed in Parulidae but now assigned to Cardinalidae on molecular evidence. Males have striking red, white, and blue-grey plumage.

children.name: Granatellus venustus
children.description: A stunning chat of Pacific slope thorn forests in western Mexico. The male has a brilliant red breast, white throat, and blue-grey upperparts with a bold white eyebrow.

children.name: Granatellus sallaei
children.description: A small, active chat of lowland forests and thickets from southern Mexico to Panama. The male has a grey throat and breast, white belly, and blue-grey upperparts.

children.name: Granatellus pelzelni
children.description: A chat of Amazonian lowland forests, with a rose-red breast, white belly, and blue-grey upperparts. It is found in river-edge forests and flooded igapó woodlands.

children.name: Cyanoloxia
children.description: A genus of South American grosbeaks and seedeaters closely related to Cyanocompsa, with deep blue males and warm brown females.

children.name: Cyanoloxia glaucocaerulea
children.description: A small, pale blue grosbeak of savanna and open woodland in south-eastern South America. The male is a soft glaucous-blue with a silvery bill.

children.name: Cyanoloxia cyanoides
children.description: A uniformly dark blue-black grosbeak with a massive bill, found in lowland forests from southern Mexico through Central America to western Ecuador.

children.name: Cyanoloxia rothschildii
children.description: A deep blue grosbeak of the Amazon basin, separated from the Blue-black Grosbeak by genetic and vocal differences. It occupies river-edge and várzea forests.

children.name: Rhodothraupis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Crimson-collared Grosbeak, a striking Mexican endemic with a massive dark bill and bold black-and-red plumage.

children.name: Rhodothraupis celaeno
children.description: A robust grosbeak endemic to north-eastern Mexico, with the male entirely black except for a brilliant crimson-red collar across the breast and nape. It inhabits subtropical forests and is classified as Least Concern.
